---
title: "dcl_core — the A=1 DCL simulation engine"
description: >-
  The Python simulation engine underlying the A=1 Discrete Causal
  Lattice series. Two engines under one import: a continuous-amplitude
  port of Paper I and an integer-token reimplementation that enforces
  A=1 by integer arithmetic.
draft: true
---

<!--
  Draft landing page for the dcl_core package. Does not render to
  the live site while `draft: true` is set.

  Unlike the paper landing pages, this is software documentation
  and updates with every release tag — bump Version, DOI, test
  status, "what is / isn't in this release" with each v0.n.0.
  Earlier versions remain citeable by their own Zenodo DOIs; the
  concept DOI resolves to the latest.
-->

## What dcl_core is

`dcl_core` is the Python simulation engine that underlies the
experiment scripts in the A=1 Discrete Causal Lattice paper
repositories. Downstream papers in the series **pin to a tagged
release** of `dcl_core` for reproducibility — the pinning string
appears below.

The package ships **two engines under a single import**, side by
side:

- **`dcl_core.core`** — a verbatim port of Paper I's
  continuous-amplitude Dirac-spinor implementation. Sessions carry
  complex `psi_R` / `psi_L` amplitudes, evolve under the bipartite
  tick rule, and enforce A=1 through a post-hop float-tolerance
  renormalisation. This is the continuous-baseline reference.
- **`dcl_core.core3d`** — the integer-token reimplementation. A
  session is a fixed budget of `n_units` indistinguishable
  probability tokens distributed across the lattice as `int64`
  fields, with continuous phases carried alongside. The analytical
  Dirac hop produces fractional new amplitudes; a Bresenham-style
  residual converts those to integer counts via a floor-plus-
  global-rebalance step that preserves the total *exactly*. A=1
  holds as a **bitwise integer identity** at every tick, not a
  float-tolerance constraint.

The two engines coexist because **Paper III** runs a direct
discrete-versus-continuous probability study, with
minimum-momentum-uncertainty experiments and Arnold-tongue
frequency-locking scans as the primary observables.

## What's in v0.1.0

<Sections to maintain at each release — update with every v0.n.0
tag.>

- Continuous-amplitude engine (`core`) — verbatim Paper I port.
- Integer-token engine (`core3d`) — Bresenham residual,
  bitwise-A=1 invariant.
- Instrumentation kit: `experiments/exp_01_throughput.py`,
  `experiments/exp_02_phase_profile.py`,
  `tools/diff_baselines.py`.

**Test status at release:** 228 passed, 4 skipped (cross-validation
tests whose bodies live downstream in Paper III), 26 xfailed
(pre-existing Paper I drift inherited verbatim with the port).

## What is deliberately not in v0.1.0

On the v0.2.0 list:

- **GPU backend's CuPy kernels.** Allocators are wired but the
  `RawKernel` bodies are stubs.
- **Pairwise multi-session interactions** — Coulomb, gauge
  coupling, emission.
- **Complex-carry switch in the residual.** The v0.1.0 default is a
  real-valued carry; whether it should be complex (tying the carry
  to the 51 unaccounted-for generators in Paper II's per-site
  automorphism algebra) is a v0.2.0+ question — expected to be
  decided by Paper III's data, not by a design preference inside
  `dcl_core`. See [`notes/bresenham_residual_design.md`](https://github.com/JackDMenendez/dcl-core/blob/v0.1.0/notes/bresenham_residual_design.md).

## Pinning for downstream consumers

Downstream paper repositories should pin to a tagged release, not
to `main`:

``` text
dcl_core @ git+https://github.com/JackDMenendez/dcl-core@v0.1.0
```

## Citation and version

- **Version:** v0.1.0
- **DOI:** [10.5281/zenodo.20350952](https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.20350952)
- **Repository:** [JackDMenendez/dcl-core](https://github.com/JackDMenendez/dcl-core)
- **GitHub release:** [v0.1.0](https://github.com/JackDMenendez/dcl-core/releases/tag/v0.1.0)

The canonical citation lives on the Zenodo deposit page; export
BibTeX / RIS / CSL from there. The concept DOI resolves to the
latest version automatically; cite the version DOI when
reproducibility matters.
